does: This paper introduces SPAC (Self-Play with Adversarial Critic), an offline preference optimization method for aligning language models using human feedback. The approach combines self-play mechanisms with an adversarial critic inspired by pessimistic offline RL techniques. The method provides theoretical convergence guarantees under single-policy concentrability assumptions in the function approximation setting, while maintaining computational scalability suitable for large language models. The core contribution bridges the gap between theoretically-justified preference optimization with formal guarantees and practically-efficient algorithms by leveraging pessimistic value estimation through adversarial interaction during training.
connects: This work builds on two converging research threads in LLM alignment and offline reinforcement learning. It extends the empirical success of preference optimization methods like DPO and IPO by grounding them in offline RL theory, specifically addressing their lack of convergence guarantees and vulnerability to sparse data coverage—classical problems in offline RL. The paper draws directly from recent pessimism-based offline RL approaches that achieve both theoretical guarantees and computational efficiency. It responds to the limitation of prior theoretically-motivated preference optimization work that lacked scalability for large models, positioning itself at the intersection of RLHF practice, offline RL foundations, and adversarial training paradigms used in language model development.
enables: This work opens new directions for provably-safe LLM alignment at scale. By demonstrating that pessimistic offline RL principles can be effectively adapted to language model preference learning, it enables further research into theoretically-grounded alignment methods without sacrificing computational efficiency. The approach suggests that self-play with adversarial critics could be a general framework for other alignment challenges beyond preference optimization. Future work could relax the concentrability assumptions, extend guarantees to more complex model classes, or combine these methods with other alignment techniques. The framework potentially enables certification and verification of alignment properties, which is critical for deploying increasingly capable language models safely.
